editions.name: Community
editions.summary: Free. Full Proxy, Repeater, Decoder, Comparer, Sequencer, and a throttled Intruder. No active scanner, no project save, no BApp Store.
editions.useWhen: Learning, CTFs, manual testing on a budget. Covers most of what you need for hands-on web pentest practice.

editions.name: Professional
editions.summary: Paid. Adds the active vulnerability scanner, full-speed Intruder, BApp Store extensions, project save/load, and Collaborator.
editions.useWhen: Real engagements where time pressure makes the active scanner and BApp Store worth the licence cost.

editions.name: Enterprise
editions.summary: Server-side automated scanner with CI/CD integration, scheduling, and centralised reporting. Not an interactive tool.
editions.useWhen: Continuous DAST across many apps from a build pipeline, not for individual researcher workflows.

tools.name: Proxy
tools.summary: The man-in-the-middle entry point. Browser traffic flows through Burp, where you can intercept, inspect, and modify every request and response.
keyFeatures: Intercept toggle — pause traffic and edit live
HTTP history — every request seen, searchable and filterable
WebSocket history — full WS frame inspection
Match-and-replace rules — auto-rewrite headers / params / bodies

tools.name: Target
tools.summary: The site map of every URL Burp has observed, plus the scope definition that filters which hosts other tools act on.
keyFeatures: Site map tree — discovered structure, status codes, content types
Scope — include / exclude rules with regex on hosts and paths
Issue definitions — built-in catalogue of what Pro's scanner looks for

tools.name: Repeater
tools.summary: Send a single request manually as many times as you want, with arbitrary edits each time. The workhorse for manual vuln verification.
keyFeatures: Multiple tabs — one per request under investigation
Send and inspect raw, params, headers, hex views
Diff side-by-side against a previous response
Render tab — preview the HTML response in-place

tools.name: Intruder
tools.summary: Parameterised fuzzer. Mark insertion points in a request, attach payloads, and run thousands of variants automatically.
keyFeatures: Four attack types: Sniper, Battering ram, Pitchfork, Cluster bomb
Payload sets — wordlists, numbers, dates, brute force, generated
Payload processing — base64, URL-encode, hash, prefix, custom
Grep-match / grep-extract — flag interesting responses by content

tools.name: Sequencer
tools.summary: Statistical randomness analysis on tokens (session IDs, CSRF tokens, password reset values).
keyFeatures: Live capture from a request
FIPS-style randomness tests — bit-level, byte-level, character-level
Effective entropy estimate — is this token actually unpredictable?

tools.name: Decoder
tools.summary: Stack of encode/decode operations: URL, HTML, base64, ASCII hex, gzip, plus common hashes.
keyFeatures: Smart decode — guesses the encoding chain
Apply multiple operations in sequence
Two-way — same panel for encoding and decoding

tools.name: Comparer
tools.summary: Word-level or byte-level diff between any two items. Essential for spotting subtle response differences in blind attacks.
keyFeatures: Compare requests, responses, or arbitrary text
Word-by-word diff highlighting
Byte-by-byte diff for binary payloads

tools.name: Logger
tools.summary: Cross-tool view of every HTTP request issued by any Burp tool — Proxy, Repeater, Intruder, Scanner — in one searchable timeline.
keyFeatures: Filter by tool, status, MIME type, search string
Replay any logged request straight into Repeater
Export to file for evidence / report appendices

tools.name: Scanner (Pro)
tools.summary: Automated active and passive vulnerability scanner. Crawls in-scope content and runs PortSwigger's audit checks.
keyFeatures: Passive analysis on every request seen
Active audit checks — XSS, SQLi, SSTI, SSRF, XXE, more
Out-of-band detection via Burp Collaborator
Issue activity panel with reproduction steps

tools.name: Extender / BApp Store
tools.summary: Plugin system. The BApp Store ships hundreds of community extensions for auth flows, JWTs, GraphQL, parameter mining, and more.
keyFeatures: Java, Python (Jython), and Ruby (JRuby) extension APIs
BApp Store — one-click install of vetted community plugins
Reload extensions without restarting Burp

intruderAttacks.name: Sniper
intruderAttacks.useCase: Test one parameter at a time across a list of payloads.
intruderAttacks.details: Single payload set; iterates payloads through each marked position one position at a time. The default — use it for finding XSS / SQLi in many params with a single wordlist.

intruderAttacks.name: Battering ram
intruderAttacks.details: Single payload set; the same payload value is placed in every marked position at once. Useful when the same value must appear in multiple fields (e.g. confirm-password forms).

intruderAttacks.name: Pitchfork
intruderAttacks.useCase: Parallel iteration of multiple correlated lists.
intruderAttacks.details: One payload set per position; Burp walks them in lockstep. Use for username:password pairs from a leak — pos 1 takes the email, pos 2 takes the matching password from the same line.

intruderAttacks.name: Cluster bomb
intruderAttacks.useCase: Cartesian product across multiple payload sets.
intruderAttacks.details: One payload set per position; tries every combination. Standard credential brute force (every user × every password). Request count balloons fast — use payload processing and grep-match to cut noise.

extensions.name: Logger++
extensions.purpose: Power user replacement for the Logger tab — better filtering, columns, and Bambdas (filter expressions).

extensions.name: Autorize
extensions.purpose: Replays every request as a second user to find broken access control / IDOR automatically.

extensions.name: JWT Editor
extensions.purpose: Decode, edit, and re-sign JSON Web Tokens; tests for alg=none, weak HMAC keys, and key confusion.

extensions.name: Param Miner
extensions.purpose: Hunts for hidden / unlinked parameters and headers via guess-and-diff — finds cache-poisoning vectors.

extensions.name: Active Scan++
extensions.purpose: Adds extra active checks (host header injection, smuggling, prototype pollution) on top of the built-in scanner.

extensions.name: Turbo Intruder
extensions.purpose: Scriptable, async high-speed Intruder — millions of requests per minute and race-condition primitives.

extensions.name: Collaborator Everywhere
extensions.purpose: Auto-injects a Collaborator payload into every outbound request to surface blind / out-of-band issues.

extensions.name: InQL
extensions.purpose: GraphQL-aware tooling — schema introspection, query generation, and a request builder.

tips: Set scope first. Out-of-scope items clutter the site map and risk hitting third parties — Target → Scope, then 'show only in-scope' everywhere.
Install Burp's CA cert in your browser (http://burp once proxied) so HTTPS pages don't throw warnings and Burp can decrypt traffic.
Use a dedicated browser profile (or the bundled Burp browser) that only ever talks to Burp — avoids leaking personal traffic into engagement projects.
Right-click → 'Send to Repeater' is the most-used shortcut. Bind Ctrl+R if you don't already have it.
Match-and-replace rules can pin a session cookie, strip CSP headers in a lab, or rewrite a Host — set them up once per engagement.
Save projects (Pro) per engagement so the site map, history, and issues persist across days. Community can't save state — script your setup with extensions instead.
Combine Logger++ filters with Bambdas for advanced queries (e.g. response contains /admin and status==200) — replaces several manual checks.
limitations: The active scanner is Professional-only. The Community Edition relies entirely on manual testing and Repeater / Intruder workflows.
Intruder in the Community Edition is heavily throttled — minutes-per-request range; turn to Turbo Intruder or curl loops for serious fuzzing.
Burp is HTTP-centric. Non-HTTP protocols (raw TCP, custom binary) need a different tool or a custom proxy extension.
Java-based GUI is heavy on RAM — large projects (hundreds of MB of HTTP history) noticeably slow down the UI.
Testing applications you do not have written authorisation to test is illegal in most jurisdictions.
